nodes.label: Aviation
nodes.summary: The whole airplane knowledge web. Click any node to expand it; dashed lines link concepts that live on different branches but drive each other. Pan by dragging the background, zoom with the wheel, and drag a node to pin it.

nodes.label: Aerodynamics
nodes.summary: How the wing and the air trade momentum to make lift, drag and control.

nodes.label: Four Forces
nodes.summary: Lift, weight, thrust and drag. In unaccelerated flight the opposing pairs are equal; any imbalance is an acceleration.

nodes.label: Lift
nodes.summary: The aerodynamic force perpendicular to the relative wind. L = ½ρV²·S·CL — set by air density, speed², wing area and the coefficient of lift (which you fly with angle of attack).

nodes.label: Angle of Attack
nodes.summary: Angle between the chord line and the relative wind. The pilot's real lift control — the wing always stalls at the same critical AoA regardless of airspeed or attitude.

nodes.label: Coefficient of Lift
nodes.summary: Dimensionless lift efficiency of the airfoil at a given AoA. Rises with AoA up to the critical angle, then collapses.

nodes.label: Bernoulli & Newton
nodes.summary: Two views of the same reality: faster flow over the camber means lower pressure (Bernoulli), and the wing deflects a mass of air downward (Newton's third law). Both are correct.

nodes.label: Ground Effect
nodes.summary: Within about one wingspan of the surface the ground interrupts downwash and wingtip vortices — induced drag drops and the wing feels more efficient. Floats on landing, tries to settle back on takeoff.

nodes.label: Stall
nodes.summary: Airflow separates from the upper wing past the critical AoA and lift drops sharply. Always an angle-of-attack event — you can stall at any airspeed or attitude.

nodes.label: Critical AoA
nodes.summary: The single angle where the boundary layer separates. Fixed for the airfoil; hitting it is what defines the stall.

nodes.label: Stall Speed (Vs)
nodes.summary: The speed that produces critical AoA in 1-g flight. Published for a clean and landing configuration; it is a symptom, not the cause.

nodes.label: Spin
nodes.summary: An aggravated stall with yaw — one wing more stalled than the other, autorotating. Recovery (PARE): Power idle, Ailerons neutral, Rudder opposite, Elevator forward.

nodes.label: Load Factor & Vs
nodes.summary: Stall speed rises with the square root of load factor: a 60° bank doubles load and raises Vs by ~41%. The "accelerated stall."

nodes.label: Drag
nodes.summary: The rearward aerodynamic force. Total drag is the sum of parasite and induced — their opposite trends with speed create the drag curve.

nodes.label: Parasite Drag
nodes.summary: Form + skin friction + interference. Grows with the square of airspeed — the fast-flight penalty.

nodes.label: Induced Drag
nodes.summary: The price of making lift — the tilt of the lift vector from downwash and tip vortices. Greatest at low speed / high AoA, falls with speed.

nodes.label: L/D max
nodes.summary: The speed where total drag is least and lift-to-drag is greatest — best glide, max endurance for a jet, and the bottom of the power curve.

nodes.label: Region of Reversed Command
nodes.summary: Slower than L/Dmax it takes MORE power to fly slower, because induced drag is climbing. The "back of the power curve" — the low-and-slow trap on approach.

nodes.label: Thrust
nodes.summary: The propeller's reaction force. Excess thrust climbs the airplane; excess power sets rate of climb.

nodes.label: Weight
nodes.summary: Gravity acting at the CG. Sets required lift, and with the CG location drives stability and control.

nodes.label: Stability & Control
nodes.summary: Whether the airplane returns to trim on its own, and how the controls change its state.

nodes.label: Static & Dynamic Stability
nodes.summary: Static = initial tendency after a disturbance; dynamic = what the motion does over time (damped, neutral or divergent). Trainers are positively stable in both.

nodes.label: Longitudinal Stability
nodes.summary: Pitch stability, governed by CG relative to the neutral point. Forward CG = more stable, heavier controls; aft CG = twitchy and eventually unrecoverable.

nodes.label: Adverse Yaw
nodes.summary: The down-aileron wing makes more lift AND more induced drag, yawing the nose away from the turn. Answered with coordinated rudder.

nodes.label: Load Factor
nodes.summary: Ratio of lift to weight (g). Climbs with bank angle in a level turn (1/cos φ) and is limited by the flight envelope.

nodes.label: Load & Stall Link
nodes.summary: Because Vs scales with √(load factor), every increase in g — a steep turn, a pull-up, a gust — raises the speed at which the wing stalls.

nodes.label: Left-Turning Tendencies
nodes.summary: Torque, P-factor, spiraling slipstream and gyroscopic precession all pull a single-engine prop plane left, strongest at high power / low speed / high AoA.

nodes.label: High-Speed & Wake
nodes.summary: Effects that show up near the edges of the envelope or behind other aircraft.

nodes.label: Wingtip Vortices
nodes.summary: Rotating air shed from a lifting wing — the core of wake turbulence. Strongest when heavy, clean and slow. Stay at or above a heavy jet's flight path and land beyond its touchdown point.

nodes.label: Mach & Compressibility
nodes.summary: As local flow approaches the speed of sound, air compresses and shock waves form — irrelevant to trainers but the ceiling for jets (Mmo, coffin corner).

nodes.label: Groundspeed & L/D
nodes.summary: Groundspeed sets pivotal altitude and glide range; L/Dmax sets best glide and endurance — the same physics behind several tasks.

nodes.label: Airspace & ATC
nodes.summary: The invisible architecture of controlled and uncontrolled air, and who talks to whom.

nodes.label: Airspace Classes
nodes.summary: A B C D E and G — each with its own entry, equipment, weather and clearance rules.

nodes.label: Class B / C / D
nodes.summary: Terminal airspace around busy airports. B needs a clearance and Mode C/ADS-B; C/D need two-way radio contact established before entry.

nodes.label: Class E & G
nodes.summary: E is controlled airspace without a tower; G is uncontrolled. The dividing floors (700/1200 AGL) set which VFR minimums apply.

nodes.label: Special Use & TFRs
nodes.summary: Prohibited, restricted, MOA, alert and warning areas — plus temporary flight restrictions. Check NOTAMs; the DC SFRA/FRZ is home turf near KANP.

nodes.label: VFR Weather Minimums
nodes.summary: The "3-152" cloud-clearance and visibility rules that keep VFR traffic able to see and avoid — they tighten as the airspace gets busier.

nodes.label: ATC Services
nodes.summary: Clearances, separation and advisories — the human layer.

nodes.label: Clearances & Readbacks
nodes.summary: An ATC clearance is an authorization, not an instruction to violate safety. Read back anything that keeps you clear of others or the ground.

nodes.label: Flight Following
nodes.summary: VFR radar advisories — traffic calls and a controller watching, workload permitting. Cheap insurance.

nodes.label: Radio Phraseology
nodes.summary: Who you are, where you are, what you want. Standard phraseology keeps a shared frequency unambiguous.

nodes.label: Airport Operations
nodes.summary: The rules and rhythm of the field itself.

nodes.label: Traffic Pattern
nodes.summary: Standard left turns unless charted otherwise: upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final. At KANP both runways are left traffic — don't infer the side from geometry.

nodes.label: Markings, Signs & Lighting
nodes.summary: Runway/taxiway paint, hold-short lines, mandatory (red) and location (black) signs, VASI/PAPI glidepath lights.

nodes.label: Non-Towered Ops
nodes.summary: Self-announce on CTAF, standard pattern, see-and-avoid — the KANP everyday.

nodes.label: Emergencies
nodes.summary: When the plan breaks — the flows, the priorities and the decisions rehearsed cold.

nodes.label: Engine & Power Loss
nodes.summary: The prop stops or falters — fly the airplane first, then work the problem on the way down.

nodes.label: Complete Power Loss
nodes.summary: Pitch for best glide, pick a field, run the flow — fuel selector, mixture rich, boost pump, mags both, primer in, carb heat — then Mayday/7700, secure, and land. Aviate the whole way.

nodes.label: Best Glide & Field Selection
nodes.summary: Trim to L/Dmax for maximum range, judge wind and slope, commit to a field early and fly a normal-looking high-key/low-key pattern to it.

nodes.label: Partial Power & Rough Running
nodes.summary: Diagnose fuel, air (carb ice), spark and mixture. Carb heat and a mixture sweep cure a surprising share of roughness — nurse it toward an airport, never trust it.

nodes.label: Engine Failure After Takeoff
nodes.summary: Low and slow with few options: lower the nose NOW to hold glide speed and land within a shallow arc ahead. The turn-back "impossible turn" stalls and spins the unwary — pick an altitude gate in advance.

nodes.label: Forced & Precautionary Landing
nodes.summary: Forced = must land now; precautionary = choosing to land while you still can (weather, fuel, lost). Brief passengers, tighten belts, fuel/mags off short final, minimum energy at touchdown.

nodes.label: Fire & Smoke
nodes.summary: The one emergency where speed beats troubleshooting — cut the source, ventilate deliberately, and land as soon as possible.

nodes.label: Engine Fire
nodes.summary: Mixture idle-cutoff, fuel selector off, cabin heat/air off, then a forced landing — do not attempt a restart. A sideslip can keep flames off the cabin.

nodes.label: Electrical Fire
nodes.summary: Acrid smoke and a hot smell: master/battery off, kill the bus, land. If systems are essential, add items back one at a time to find the culprit.

nodes.label: Cabin Fire
nodes.summary: Extinguisher (PASS), then ventilate — a fire fed by fresh air may mean closing vents first. Descend and land immediately; smoke is as dangerous as flame.

nodes.label: Carbon Monoxide
nodes.summary: Odorless exhaust from a cracked heater shroud — headache, sleepiness, then incapacitation. Heater off, fresh-air vents open, oxygen if available, and land.

nodes.label: System & Instrument Failures
nodes.summary: Something quits — recognize the pattern, work around it, and land where help is.

nodes.label: Electrical Failure
nodes.summary: Alternator warning / discharge: reduce load, run the checklist, and treat the battery as a countdown to essential-only. Know exactly what you lose when it goes dark.

nodes.label: Vacuum / Partial Panel
nodes.summary: Gyros die slowly and lie convincingly — the classic killer. Cover them, fly the electric turn coordinator + ASI + altimeter, and get to VFR.

nodes.label: Pitot-Static Failures
nodes.summary: A blocked pitot or static port gives a predictable but deadly-wrong reading — cross-check attitude + power, select the alternate static source, and trust the picture that agrees.

nodes.label: Gear & Flap Malfunction
nodes.summary: Gear that won't extend (manual/emergency extension, positive-g yaw), or asymmetric/split flaps — fly the airplane, run the checklist, and plan a longer, flatter arrival.

nodes.label: Trim & Autopilot Runaway
nodes.summary: A runaway trim or misbehaving autopilot: disconnect it (autopilot switch, then its breaker), hold the airplane with muscle, and re-trim by hand.

nodes.label: Loss of Control & Upset
nodes.summary: The airplane departs controlled flight or points somewhere alarming — recover with the wings first.

nodes.label: Stall / Spin Recovery
nodes.summary: Stall: reduce AoA (nose down), full power, level the wings, minimum altitude loss. Spin: PARE — Power idle, Ailerons neutral, Rudder opposite the spin, Elevator forward.

nodes.label: Unusual Attitude Recovery
nodes.summary: Nose-high near stall: add power, unload, level the wings. Nose-low overspeed: reduce power, level the wings, ease out. Bank before pitch, and believe the instruments.

nodes.label: Spiral Dive
nodes.summary: Airspeed and bank increasing, altitude unwinding: power idle, roll the wings level FIRST, then gently raise the nose — pulling before leveling only tightens it.

nodes.label: Inadvertent VFR-into-IMC
nodes.summary: The deadliest trap: commit to the instruments, a standard-rate 180° turn back toward VMC, resist the leans, and ask for help early.

nodes.label: Weather & Environmental
nodes.summary: Getting out of conditions the airplane can't survive in.

nodes.label: Inadvertent Icing Encounter
nodes.summary: Without known-ice gear: pitot heat on, exit the conditions — climb to warmer air or descend below the freezing level, add power and speed, and carry extra on approach; never let it stall.

nodes.label: Storm / Severe Turbulence
nodes.summary: If trapped: slow to maneuvering speed, hold attitude (not altitude), lights up, avoid abrupt inputs, and turn to the shortest way out.

nodes.label: Wind Shear / Microburst Escape
nodes.summary: On approach: full power, pitch to the attitude/flight-director for maximum climb, don't chase airspeed, and go around early. Never try to fly through a microburst.

nodes.label: Lost & Disoriented
nodes.summary: The five Cs: Climb (for radio/radar/nav range), Communicate, Confess, Comply, Conserve. Use flight following, DF steers and every nav aid before fuel decides for you.

nodes.label: Cabin & Passengers
nodes.summary: The emergencies that happen behind the panel.

nodes.label: Door or Window Open
nodes.summary: Startling noise, rarely a real handling problem — fly the airplane, don't grab for it in a critical phase, and land to secure it. Most cabin doors can't be closed in flight.

nodes.label: Passenger Incapacitation
nodes.summary: Brief passengers before flight; if someone is ill or panicking, delegate tasks, use medical help relayed via ATC, and land at the nearest suitable airport.

nodes.label: Hypoxia / Depressurization
nodes.summary: Insidious oxygen starvation: don oxygen, descend to a safe altitude immediately, and treat any unexplained euphoria or fumbling as hypoxia until proven otherwise.

nodes.label: Emergency Comms & Equipment
nodes.summary: Getting help, and the gear that summons it.

nodes.label: Distress vs Urgency
nodes.summary: MAYDAY×3 = distress (grave, immediate danger); PAN-PAN×3 = urgency (a problem, not yet dire). State who, what, where, intentions, and souls/fuel.

nodes.label: Squawk & 121.5
nodes.summary: Transponder 7700 (general emergency), 7600 (lost comms), 7500 (hijack); 121.5 is the guard frequency monitored everywhere. ATC can help even before you've reached them.

nodes.label: ATC Assistance & DF Steer
nodes.summary: ATC can give vectors, no-gyro steers, weather avoidance, light-gun signals and priority handling — declaring an emergency unlocks all of it. Ask, and accept the help.

nodes.label: ELT & Survival
nodes.summary: 406 MHz ELTs alert satellites with a GPS position; know how to trigger it manually, and carry what you'd need to be found and to wait for rescue.

nodes.label: Priorities & Checklists
nodes.summary: The discipline that makes all of the above survivable.

nodes.label: Aviate, Navigate, Communicate
nodes.summary: In that order, always. Fly the airplane first; a radio call or a checklist that steals attention from flying is how a survivable problem becomes a crash.

nodes.label: Memory Items vs Read-Do
nodes.summary: A handful of immediate actions are done from memory (engine fire, stall); everything else is read-and-do off the checklist once the airplane is stable and time allows.

nodes.label: Decision-Making Under Pressure
nodes.summary: Buy time (climb, hold, circle), name the problem, weigh the options, decide — then commit. Startle and tunnel vision are the real enemies.

nodes.label: Airframe & Flight Controls
nodes.summary: The structure that carries the loads and the surfaces the pilot moves to steer it.

nodes.label: Structure & Loads
nodes.summary: How the airframe is built and how flight loads travel through it to the ground and back.

nodes.label: Fuselage Construction
nodes.summary: Truss (welded tube), monocoque (skin carries all load) and semi-monocoque (skin + stringers + bulkheads) — the near-universal metal airplane.

nodes.label: Wing Structure
nodes.summary: Spars carry bending, ribs give the airfoil shape, stringers and skin carry the rest. Cantilever (internal spar) vs strut-braced; a "wet wing" is a sealed integral tank.

nodes.label: Empennage
nodes.summary: The tail: vertical stabilizer + rudder for yaw, horizontal stabilizer + elevator (or a one-piece stabilator) for pitch. It provides the stabilizing tail-down force.

nodes.label: Load Paths & Fatigue
nodes.summary: Limit load = the most you should ever pull; ultimate = 1.5× that before failure. Repeated cycles fatigue metal — hence life limits and inspections.

nodes.label: Primary Flight Controls
nodes.summary: The three that move the airplane about its three axes — roll, pitch and yaw.

nodes.label: Ailerons — Roll
nodes.summary: Move opposite each other to bank about the longitudinal axis. The down-going aileron adds lift and drag, so they generate adverse yaw.

nodes.label: Elevator / Stabilator — Pitch
nodes.summary: Change the tail's angle of attack to pitch about the lateral axis. A stabilator is an all-moving surface with an anti-servo tab to add feel and prevent over-control.

nodes.label: Rudder — Yaw
nodes.summary: Controls yaw about the vertical axis — it coordinates turns and counters adverse yaw and P-factor. It is not what turns the airplane.

nodes.label: Secondary Controls & High Lift
nodes.summary: Trim to relieve pressure, and the devices that reshape the wing for slow flight.

nodes.label: Trim Systems
nodes.summary: Trim tabs, anti-servo tabs, servo tabs or a movable stabilizer relieve the control-pressure so the pilot doesn't hold force. Set for hands-off flight at a target speed.

nodes.label: Flaps
nodes.summary: Plain, split, slotted and Fowler flaps raise the coefficient of lift (and drag), lowering stall speed so you can approach slower and steeper.

nodes.label: Leading-Edge Devices
nodes.summary: Slots and slats delay separation to a higher AoA for more usable lift; on jets they extend the low-speed envelope. Spoilers/speedbrakes do the opposite — kill lift and add drag.

nodes.label: Control Mechanics
nodes.summary: How yoke and pedals actually move the surfaces, and what limits how fast you may fly.

nodes.label: Cables, Pushrods & Bellcranks
nodes.summary: Yoke and pedals drive the surfaces through cables, pushrods, bellcranks and stops. Control checks confirm "free and correct" before every flight.

nodes.label: Flutter & Balance
nodes.summary: Surfaces are mass- and aerodynamically balanced so they don't flutter — a destructive resonance that Vne is set below. Never exceed the red line, especially in rough air.

nodes.label: Landing Gear & Brakes
nodes.summary: What holds the airplane up on the ground and stops it.

nodes.label: Fixed vs Retractable
nodes.summary: Tricycle gear is stable and easy on the ground; tailwheel demands active directional control. Retractable gear cuts drag but adds a "gear-down" item that must never be skipped.

nodes.label: Brakes & Tires
nodes.summary: Independent hydraulic disc brakes give differential steering; hydroplaning on a wet runway and overheating on hard braking are the traps.

nodes.label: Shock Absorption
nodes.summary: Oleo (air/oil) struts, spring-steel legs or bungees absorb the touchdown and taxi loads — the reason a firm arrival isn't a structural one.

nodes.label: Human Factors
nodes.summary: The pilot as the least predictable system — decision-making, physiology and illusions.

nodes.label: Aeronautical Decision Making
nodes.summary: A systematic approach to the mental side of flying: recognize hazards, weigh risk, decide, act, and evaluate — before the airplane decides for you.

nodes.label: Hazardous Attitudes
nodes.summary: Anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho, resignation — each with a spoken antidote. Naming yours is half the cure.

nodes.label: Risk Management (PAVE / 5P)
nodes.summary: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures — and the 5P checkpoints (Plan, Plane, Pilot, Passengers, Programming) revisited in flight.

nodes.label: IMSAFE & Personal Minimums
nodes.summary: Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Eating/Emotion — a self-check against limits you set on the ground, not in the air.

nodes.label: Aeromedical Factors
nodes.summary: How altitude and stress act on the body.

nodes.label: Hypoxia
nodes.summary: Oxygen starvation — insidious, euphoric, deadly. Regulatory oxygen above 12,500 ft (after 30 min) and always above 14,000 ft.

nodes.label: Four Types
nodes.summary: Hypoxic (low ambient O₂ / altitude), hypemic (blood can't carry it — CO, anemia), stagnant (blood not moving — g, cold), histotoxic (cells can't use it — alcohol).

nodes.label: Symptoms & TUC
nodes.summary: Euphoria, poor judgment, tunnel vision, cyanosis — and no alarm from the pilot. Time of useful consciousness collapses with altitude: minutes at 18,000, seconds above 30,000.

nodes.label: Supplemental Oxygen (91.211)
nodes.summary: Crew O₂ above 12,500 ft after 30 min and continuously above 14,000 ft; passengers offered above 15,000 ft. Cabin altitude, not aircraft altitude, is what counts.

nodes.label: Hyperventilation
nodes.summary: Over-breathing from stress blows off CO₂ — tingling, dizziness, mimics hypoxia. Slow the breathing, talk, breathe into a bag.

nodes.label: Spatial Disorientation
nodes.summary: The inner ear lies without outside references — the vestibular and visual systems disagree and the pilot loses the horizon.

nodes.label: The Leans
nodes.summary: A slow roll goes unfelt, so rolling level feels like a bank the other way. The most common illusion — believe the attitude indicator, not the seat.

nodes.label: Graveyard Spiral
nodes.summary: In a prolonged turn the sensation of turning fades; correcting the resulting altitude loss by pulling only tightens the descending spiral.

nodes.label: Somatogravic Illusion
nodes.summary: Forward acceleration (a go-around, takeoff into a black hole) feels like a pitch-up, tempting a dangerous push-over. Deceleration feels like a dive.

nodes.label: Coriolis Illusion
nodes.summary: Moving your head during a turn tumbles the fluid in all three canals at once — an overwhelming false rotation. Keep head movements slow in IMC.

nodes.label: Carbon Monoxide
nodes.summary: Odorless exhaust gas from a cracked heater shroud — headache, sleepiness. Cabin heat off, vents open, land.

nodes.label: Fatigue & IMSAFE Physiology
nodes.summary: Acute vs chronic fatigue erodes judgment and reaction like alcohol; add hypoglycemia, dehydration and stress. The body's limits belong on the ground checklist.

nodes.label: Vision & Night
nodes.summary: Rods for night/peripheral, off-center viewing, 30-min dark adaptation, and the black-hole approach illusion.

nodes.label: Optical Illusions on Landing
nodes.summary: Runway width, up/down-sloping terrain, and featureless approaches distort your perceived glidepath — cross-check the VASI/PAPI and the numbers.

nodes.label: Communication & CRM
nodes.summary: Single-pilot resource management: use every resource — ATC, checklists, automation, passengers — and communicate clearly.

nodes.label: Communication
nodes.summary: Clear, standard, timely radio and crew communication — the thread that ties ATC, CRM and emergencies together.

nodes.label: Instruments & IFR
nodes.summary: The six-pack, the glass, and flying by reference to them in cloud.

nodes.label: Pitot-Static Instruments
nodes.summary: Airspeed, altimeter and vertical speed — all fed by ram and static air. Understand each blockage failure by heart.

nodes.label: Airspeed Indicator
nodes.summary: Difference between pitot (ram) and static pressure. Blocked pitot with open drain reads zero; blocked pitot+drain acts like an altimeter in a climb/descent.

nodes.label: Altimeter
nodes.summary: Aneroid measuring static pressure against a set datum (Kollsman window). "High to low, look out below" — set the local setting.

nodes.label: Vertical Speed Indicator
nodes.summary: Rate of static-pressure change — trend plus rate, with a lag.

nodes.label: Gyroscopic Instruments
nodes.summary: Rigidity and precession put to work: attitude, heading and turn rate — spun by vacuum or electricity.

nodes.label: Attitude Indicator
nodes.summary: The artificial horizon — pitch and bank at a glance, the master instrument in IMC.

nodes.label: Heading Indicator
nodes.summary: A stable gyro heading with no compass errors — but it drifts, so reset it to the mag compass every ~15 min.

nodes.label: Turn Coordinator
nodes.summary: Rate of turn and, via the inclinometer ball, coordination. Electric — your standby when vacuum quits.

nodes.label: Glass Cockpit & Automation
nodes.summary: PFD/MFD, AHRS, ADC, autopilot and moving map — more information, new failure modes, and the automation-management workload.

nodes.label: PFD & MFD
nodes.summary: The primary flight display paints attitude, air data and navigation on one screen; the multi-function display carries the moving map, engine and systems pages.

nodes.label: AHRS & ADC
nodes.summary: Solid-state boxes replace spinning gyros: the AHRS derives attitude and heading, the air data computer derives airspeed, altitude and VSI from the pitot-static system.

nodes.label: Autopilot & Flight Director
nodes.summary: The flight director commands pitch/roll bars to follow; the autopilot flies them. Know the modes armed vs captured — mode confusion is a modern killer.

nodes.label: GPS / FMS Navigator
nodes.summary: The IFR navigator sequences the flight plan, loads and flies approaches, and drives the CDI/autopilot. Verify the active leg and the approach loaded, every time.

nodes.label: Automation Management
nodes.summary: Choose the right level of automation for the workload, cross-check what the box is doing against raw data, and be ready to hand-fly when it surprises you.

nodes.label: Instrument Flying
nodes.summary: Controlling the airplane and navigating solely by reference to instruments when the world outside disappears — the whole IFR discipline, gate to gate.

nodes.label: Attitude Instrument Flying
nodes.summary: Flying precise attitudes off the panel instead of the horizon — the motor skill under everything else in IMC.

nodes.label: Control & Performance Method
nodes.summary: Set a known attitude + power on the control instruments (AI, tach/MP), then check the result on the performance instruments (ASI, ALT, VSI, HI) and trim it off.

nodes.label: Primary & Supporting Instruments
nodes.summary: In each phase one instrument is "primary" for pitch, bank and power (e.g. straight-and-level: ALT primary pitch, HI primary bank), the rest support it. Interpretation, not staring.

nodes.label: The Scan & Its Errors
nodes.summary: A disciplined cross-check across the panel. Fixation (locking on one), omission (ignoring one) and emphasis (over-trusting one) are the three ways the scan breaks down.

nodes.label: Partial Panel & Unusual Attitudes
nodes.summary: Lose the AI/HI and fly the electric turn coordinator, ASI and altimeter; recover unusual attitudes by the ASI trend — power/pitch/bank — reading the raw instruments, not your ears.

nodes.label: Clearance & Departure
nodes.summary: Getting into the system and safely off the ground into the clag.

nodes.label: The Clearance (CRAFT)
nodes.summary: Clearance limit, Route, Altitude, Frequency, Transponder — copied, read back, and flown exactly as issued until amended.

nodes.label: Void Times & Release
nodes.summary: From a non-towered field ATC issues a clearance void time and often a hold-for-release — be airborne before the void time or the clearance evaporates.

nodes.label: ODP vs SID
nodes.summary: Obstacle Departure Procedures protect you from terrain and are pilot-selected; Standard Instrument Departures are ATC routings. Know which applies before you roll.

nodes.label: Climb Gradients & the Trouble-T
nodes.summary: The plate's trouble-T flags a non-standard takeoff minimum or a required climb gradient (ft/NM) — convert it to fpm at your groundspeed and confirm you can make it.

nodes.label: Enroute
nodes.summary: Navigating the IFR structure between departure and approach.

nodes.label: Airways & Fixes
nodes.summary: Victor airways (VOR) and T-routes (GPS) connect fixes and intersections. Fly the centerline; RNAV lets you go direct where terrain and airspace allow.

nodes.label: IFR Altitudes
nodes.summary: MEA (guaranteed nav + obstacle), MOCA (obstacle, nav within 22 NM), MRA (reception), MCA (cross at/above), and OROCA off-route terrain clearance — the alphabet that keeps you high enough.

nodes.label: Position Reporting
nodes.summary: In non-radar or on request: name, position, time, altitude, next fix + ETA, and the fix after. Plus the mandatory reports ATC never has to ask for.

nodes.label: Descent & STAR
nodes.summary: Standard Terminal Arrival Routes and descent clearances (pilot's vs ATC's discretion, "descend via") bridge enroute to the approach — plan the top of descent early.

nodes.label: Holding
nodes.summary: Parking in the sky on a protected racetrack when the system needs you to wait.

nodes.label: Entry Types
nodes.summary: Direct, parallel and teardrop entries, chosen by your inbound heading relative to the holding course (the ±70° rule). Visualize the pattern before you arrive at the fix.

nodes.label: Timing & Leg Lengths
nodes.summary: Standard right turns, 1-minute legs at/below 14,000 ft (1½ above), or a DME/RNAV leg length. Start timing abeam the fix or wings-level, whichever is later.

nodes.label: Wind Correction in the Hold
nodes.summary: Drift on the outbound leg is corrected by tripling the inbound crab, and outbound timing is adjusted to make the inbound leg come out right.

nodes.label: EFC & Holding Fuel
nodes.summary: Expect-Further-Clearance time is your lost-comms trigger; watch holding fuel against your alternate + reserve and speak up before it bites.

nodes.label: Instrument Approaches
nodes.summary: The descent from the enroute structure to a runway you can't see until the end — the highest-workload minutes in IFR.

nodes.label: Precision vs Non-Precision
nodes.summary: Precision/APV (ILS, LPV, LNAV/VNAV) give vertical guidance to a Decision Altitude; non-precision (LOC, VOR, LNAV, RNP) step you down to a Minimum Descent Altitude.

nodes.label: Approach Segments
nodes.summary: Feeder → Initial (IAF) → Intermediate (IF) → Final (FAF) → Missed (MAP). Each segment has its own obstacle clearance and altitude.

nodes.label: Minimums: DA vs MDA
nodes.summary: At the DA you decide and may momentarily descend below in the go-around; at the MDA you must not descend until the runway environment and required visibility are in sight.

nodes.label: Landing from the Approach (91.175)
nodes.summary: Below MDA/DA needs three things: flight visibility at or above minimums, a normal descent to the intended runway, and a listed visual reference (threshold, TDZ, VASI, lights…).

nodes.label: The Missed Approach
nodes.summary: Not in sight, or unstable, at the MAP/DA → cram, climb, clean, and fly the published miss (or ATC's). Brief it before you start down so it's a reflex, not a decision.

nodes.label: Circling Approach
nodes.summary: Maneuvering visually to a runway not aligned with the final course, at circling MDA, staying within the protected radius for your approach category and never descending until in the slot.

nodes.label: IFR Regulations & Currency
nodes.summary: The rules that decide whether you and the airplane may legally launch into the system today.

nodes.label: Currency: 6-HITS & IPC
nodes.summary: In the last 6 months: 6 approaches, Holding, Intercepting and Tracking courses — in actual or a view-limiting device. Lapse it and you need an Instrument Proficiency Check.

nodes.label: Alternate Requirements (1-2-3)
nodes.summary: File an alternate unless, ±1 hr of ETA, the ceiling is ≥2,000 ft and visibility ≥3 SM. Alternate minimums default 600-2 (precision) / 800-2 (non-precision) unless charted otherwise.

nodes.label: IFR Fuel Requirements
nodes.summary: Enough to reach the destination, then the alternate, then 45 minutes at normal cruise (91.167) — the number that sizes the whole flight.

nodes.label: Lost Communications
nodes.summary: Squawk 7600, continue VFR if able; if IMC fly Route (Assigned→Vectored→Expected→Filed) and Altitude (highest of Assigned, MEA, Expected), and arrive per your clearance/EFC.

nodes.label: Required Reports to ATC
nodes.summary: Reports ATC never has to ask for: leaving an altitude, unable to climb/descend 500 fpm, missed approach, loss of nav/comm, unforecast weather, safety of flight, and more.

nodes.label: The IFR Environment
nodes.summary: The conditions that make instrument flight necessary — and dangerous.

nodes.label: IMC, VMC & VFR-into-IMC
nodes.summary: Instrument vs visual meteorological conditions. A VFR pilot continuing into IMC is one of aviation's deadliest chains — the case for the rating and the 180° turn.

nodes.label: Structural Icing & IFR
nodes.summary: IFR routinely means cloud near freezing. Without known-ice equipment, an IFR flight plan is not a license to enter icing — plan the escape (warmer air, lower, out).

nodes.label: Ceiling, Visibility & RVR
nodes.summary: Reported ceiling and visibility (or runway visual range) decide whether the approach is even legal to start and continue — and RVR governs where it's available.

nodes.label: Flight Maneuvers
nodes.summary: The stick-and-rudder skills, from the first takeoff to the checkride tasks.

nodes.label: Basic Airwork
nodes.summary: The building blocks flown to ACS tolerances.

nodes.label: Straight & Level / Trim
nodes.summary: Hold heading and altitude by attitude + power, trimming off the pressure. The reference for everything else.

nodes.label: Climbs, Descents & Turns
nodes.summary: Coordinated entries and rollouts, pitch for speed, power for rate, rudder to keep the ball centered.

nodes.label: Slow Flight & Stalls
nodes.summary: Flight at high AoA near the stall, and power-on/power-off stall recognition and recovery — the feel of the wing running out of margin.

nodes.label: Steep Turns
nodes.summary: 45–50° banked level turns holding altitude — a lesson in load factor, back-pressure and coordinated rudder.

nodes.label: Ground Reference
nodes.summary: Flying precise tracks over the ground while the wind tries to blow you off them.

nodes.label: Turns Around a Point / S-Turns
nodes.summary: Vary bank to hold a constant-radius track — steepest downwind, shallowest upwind. Pure wind-drift correction.

nodes.label: Eights on Pylons
nodes.summary: Hold the wingtip pinned on a pylon by flying at pivotal altitude (≈GS²/11.3) — elevator, not aileron, chases the pylon. (See the interactive explainer.)

nodes.label: Takeoffs & Landings
nodes.summary: Normal, crosswind, short-field and soft-field variants — the parts of every flight most likely to bite.

nodes.label: Crosswind Technique
nodes.summary: Wing-low slip or crab-to-kick: aileron into the wind, opposite rudder to align with the runway. Limited by control authority and the demonstrated crosswind.

nodes.label: Short & Soft Field
nodes.summary: Short-field: max performance over an obstacle at Vx. Soft-field: weight off the wheels early, in ground effect, never stopping on soft ground.

nodes.label: Stabilized Approach & Go-Around
nodes.summary: On speed, on glidepath, configured and trimmed by a gate altitude — or go around. The decision that prevents most landing accidents.

nodes.label: Emergency Airwork
nodes.summary: Best glide, engine-out field selection and the flows that live in muscle memory.

nodes.label: Navigation
nodes.summary: Knowing where you are, where you're going, and how the wind and Earth bend the path.

nodes.label: Pilotage & Dead Reckoning
nodes.summary: Navigate by looking outside at charted landmarks (pilotage) and by computing heading/time/fuel from known wind and speed (dead reckoning).

nodes.label: Wind Correction / Triangle
nodes.summary: The wind triangle resolves true airspeed + wind into ground track and groundspeed, giving the crab angle (WCA) that holds course.

nodes.label: True–Magnetic–Compass
nodes.summary: True course from the chart, corrected by variation to magnetic, then by deviation to compass. "East is least, west is best."

nodes.label: Magnetic Compass Errors
nodes.summary: Variation, deviation, dip: it leads/lags on turns (UNOS) and swings with acceleration (ANDS) — only trustworthy in steady, level, unaccelerated flight.

nodes.label: Charts
nodes.summary: The paper (and glass) picture of the world below and the system above.

nodes.label: Sectional / TAC
nodes.summary: VFR charts: terrain, obstacles, airspace, airports and frequencies at 1:500,000 (sectional) or 1:250,000 (terminal area).

nodes.label: IFR Enroute & Approach Plates
nodes.summary: Low/high enroute charts show airways, MEAs and fixes; instrument approach procedures show how to descend to a runway in the clag.

nodes.label: Radio Navigation
nodes.summary: Ground- and space-based ways to fix position.

nodes.label: VOR
nodes.summary: VHF omnidirectional range — fly TO or FROM a station along any of 360 radials. Check with a VOT or dual-VOR; line-of-sight only.

nodes.label: Radials & the OBS
nodes.summary: The station broadcasts 360 radials; the OBS selects a course and the CDI shows deflection left/right of it — independent of aircraft heading.

nodes.label: TO / FROM & Reverse Sensing
nodes.summary: The flag tells you which side of the station the selected course points to. Fly a FROM course with a TO flag and the needle senses backward — the classic trap.

nodes.label: Service Volumes
nodes.summary: Terminal, Low and High altitude VORs each guarantee accuracy only within charted range/altitude limits — line-of-sight, so range grows with altitude.

nodes.label: Accuracy Checks
nodes.summary: VOT (±4°), designated ground/airborne checkpoints (±4°/±6°), or dual-VOR cross-check (±4°) — logged for IFR currency of the receiver.

nodes.label: GPS / RNAV / WAAS
nodes.summary: Satellite navigation with RAIM/WAAS integrity, enabling area navigation, LPV approaches near ILS minima, and direct routing.

nodes.label: RAIM & WAAS Integrity
nodes.summary: RAIM uses extra satellites to self-check position; WAAS adds ground-corrected accuracy and vertical guidance, replacing the RAIM check for approaches.

nodes.label: RNAV Approach Minima
nodes.summary: One RNAV plate, several lines: LNAV (lateral only), LNAV/VNAV and LPV (vertical guidance) — LPV gets you near ILS 200-ft minimums.

nodes.label: Database & Substitution
nodes.summary: A current 28-day database is required to fly the procedures; GPS may substitute for DME and ADF, and for some navaids, per the AIM.

nodes.label: ILS
nodes.summary: Localizer (lateral) + glideslope (vertical) precision approach down to 200 ft and ½ SM — the gold standard.

nodes.label: Localizer
nodes.summary: A narrow (~5°) course to the runway centerline — four times more sensitive than a VOR. Usable as a non-precision approach on its own.

nodes.label: Glideslope
nodes.summary: A ~3° descent path from a separate transmitter. Beware false glideslopes above the true one and never chase a flagged needle.

nodes.label: Marker Beacons & Categories
nodes.summary: Outer/middle markers (or DME/fixes) mark distance; CAT I/II/III define how low certified crews and equipment may go.

nodes.label: DME & Transponder
nodes.summary: DME gives slant-range distance; the transponder + ADS-B paints you for ATC and traffic.

nodes.label: DME & Slant Range
nodes.summary: Measures line-of-sight distance to the station, not ground distance — the error is largest overhead and at altitude.

nodes.label: Transponder & Modes
nodes.summary: Mode A (code), Mode C (altitude), Mode S (data). Codes: 1200 VFR, 7500/7600/7700 for hijack/lost-comms/emergency.

nodes.label: ADS-B Out & In
nodes.summary: ADS-B Out broadcasts your GPS position for surveillance (required in most controlled airspace); ADS-B In brings free traffic (TIS-B) and weather (FIS-B).

nodes.label: Flight Planning
nodes.summary: Turning a departure and a destination into a flyable route with times, fuel and outs.

nodes.label: Cross-Country Planning
nodes.summary: Choose a route and checkpoints, clear terrain and airspace, pick fuel stops, and check the whole picture — weather, NOTAMs, runway lengths and performance.

nodes.label: E6B / Flight Computer
nodes.summary: The mechanical or app flight computer solves the wind triangle, converts IAS↔TAS, computes time-speed-distance and fuel, and handles unit and density-altitude problems.

nodes.label: Time, Speed, Distance & Fuel
nodes.summary: Leg groundspeeds give leg times; fuel burn plus reserves (VFR 30 min day / 45 min night, IFR to destination + alternate + 45 min) sizes the tanks.

nodes.label: Descent Planning
nodes.summary: The 3-to-1 rule: ~3 NM per 1,000 ft to lose. Start down early enough for a comfortable, stabilized arrival rather than a dive at the field.

nodes.label: Diversion & Lost Procedures
nodes.summary: Divert to a pre-briefed alternate on heading and time; if lost, the 5 Cs — Climb, Communicate, Confess, Comply, Conserve — and use every nav aid you have.

nodes.label: VFR Flight Plan & Flight Following
nodes.summary: File and open a VFR flight plan for search-and-rescue coverage (it is NOT ATC), and separately request flight following for radar traffic advisories.

nodes.label: ADS-B
nodes.summary: Automatic Dependent Surveillance–Broadcast: your position, out to ATC and other traffic; in gives free traffic and weather (FIS-B).

nodes.label: Performance & Weight-Balance
nodes.summary: What the airplane can actually do today, and whether it's loaded to do it safely.

nodes.label: Takeoff & Landing Performance
nodes.summary: Ground roll and distance over a 50-ft obstacle from the POH charts — corrected for density altitude, weight, wind, slope and surface.

nodes.label: Climb Performance
nodes.summary: Excess thrust vs excess power: Vx (best angle, most altitude per distance — obstacle) vs Vy (best rate, most altitude per time).

nodes.label: Vx vs Vy
nodes.summary: Vx clears the trees; Vy gets you up fastest. They converge with altitude and meet at the absolute ceiling.

nodes.label: Service & Absolute Ceiling
nodes.summary: Service ceiling = 100 fpm climb remains; absolute = climb goes to zero. Both fall as weight and density altitude rise.

nodes.label: Weight & Balance
nodes.summary: Total weight within limits AND the CG within the envelope. Moment = weight × arm; sum moments, divide by weight, find the CG.

nodes.label: Center of Gravity
nodes.summary: The balance point. Forward CG: stable, higher stall speed, more elevator needed to flare. Aft CG: less stable, lighter controls, worse spin recovery.

nodes.label: Loading & Envelope
nodes.summary: Plot the loaded point on the CG envelope; shift baggage or fuel to stay inside for the whole flight as fuel burns.

nodes.label: V-Speeds & the Envelope
nodes.summary: The alphabet of airspeeds — Vso, Vs1, Vx, Vy, Va, Vno, Vne, Vfe — and the V-n diagram that bounds load factor against speed.

nodes.label: Maneuvering Speed (Va)
nodes.summary: Below Va the wing stalls before it overstresses — full deflection is structurally safe. Va decreases as weight decreases.

nodes.label: Airspeed Colour Arcs
nodes.summary: White (flap range), green (normal), yellow (caution, smooth air only), red line (never exceed) on the ASI.

nodes.label: Powerplant & Systems
nodes.summary: The engine and the airframe systems that keep it, and you, running.

nodes.label: Reciprocating Engine
nodes.summary: The typical four-stroke, air-cooled, horizontally-opposed piston engine: intake, compression, power, exhaust.

nodes.label: Four-Stroke Cycle
nodes.summary: Intake, compression, power, exhaust — two crank revolutions per power stroke. Valves, piston and ignition all timed to the crankshaft.

nodes.label: Ignition (Magnetos)
nodes.summary: Two engine-driven magnetos fire two plugs per cylinder — redundant and independent of the electrical system. The mag check finds a dead plug or bad timing.

nodes.label: Dual Ignition & the Mag Check
nodes.summary: Two plugs per cylinder burn the charge faster and more completely, and give redundancy. Runup drop shows plug/lead health; a rise to BOTH confirms.

nodes.label: Impulse Coupling
nodes.summary: A spring-and-flyweight device that retards and boosts the spark for starting, then releases at RPM — why the left mag usually carries the starting circuit.

nodes.label: Carburetor & Icing
nodes.summary: Venturi + fuel discharge. Fuel vaporization and pressure drop can freeze the venturi even at +20 °C in visible moisture — carb heat is the cure and the prevention.

nodes.label: Carb Ice Symptoms
nodes.summary: Fixed-pitch: gradual RPM drop then rough running. Constant-speed: manifold-pressure drop. Applying heat first roughens (melt-through) then smooths.

nodes.label: Carb Heat
nodes.summary: Routes unfiltered, heated air around the exhaust into the induction. Use it as prevention on descent and low power; it enriches the mixture and costs some power.

nodes.label: Fuel Injection
nodes.summary: Meters fuel to each cylinder — no carb ice, but vapor-lock and hot-start quirks instead.

nodes.label: Detonation & Preignition
nodes.summary: Detonation = uncontrolled explosion of the charge (lean/hot/low-octane); preignition = charge lit early by a hot spot. Both destroy engines — manage with mixture, cowl flaps and correct fuel.

nodes.label: Detonation
nodes.summary: The charge explodes instead of burning smoothly — from low-grade fuel, over-lean high-power, or high CHT. Enrich, reduce power, open cowl flaps, descend.

nodes.label: Preignition
nodes.summary: The charge lights before the plug fires, on a glowing hot spot (deposit, cracked plug). Often follows detonation; symptoms overlap — cool the cylinder.

nodes.label: Mixture & Leaning
nodes.summary: Air is thinner with altitude, so the fuel/air ratio must be leaned to hold the right mixture, peak EGT and economy — and enriched before descent.

nodes.label: EGT & Peak
nodes.summary: Exhaust gas temperature peaks at the chemically correct mixture. Lean-of-peak runs cool and economical; rich-of-peak runs cooler and makes more power.

nodes.label: Best Power vs Best Economy
nodes.summary: Slightly rich of peak = most power (climb); at or lean of peak = best fuel flow (cruise). Full rich for takeoff at low density altitude.

nodes.label: Lubrication & Cooling
nodes.summary: Oil lubricates, cools, cleans, seals and cushions; cooling fins and baffles carry the rest of the heat away. Watch oil temp/pressure and CHT together.

nodes.label: Oil System
nodes.summary: Wet or dry sump: pump, filter, cooler, pressure and temperature gauges. Low pressure + high temp = get it on the ground.

nodes.label: Cooling & Cowl Flaps
nodes.summary: Air-cooled via baffles and fins; cowl flaps trade cooling for drag. Shock cooling from a fast idle descent cracks cylinders.

nodes.label: Propeller
nodes.summary: An airfoil that converts engine power into thrust. Twist gives a near-constant angle of attack along the blade.

nodes.label: Fixed vs Constant-Speed
nodes.summary: Fixed-pitch is a compromise; a constant-speed prop uses a governor to hold RPM by changing blade angle — throttle sets manifold pressure (power), prop lever sets RPM.

nodes.label: The Governor
nodes.summary: Flyweights and a pilot valve meter oil to the prop hub to hold the selected RPM as load changes — on-speed, under-speed, over-speed.

nodes.label: MP + RPM Order
nodes.summary: Increasing power: prop (RPM) up first, then throttle (MP). Reducing: throttle back first, then prop — to avoid high MP on low RPM.

nodes.label: P-Factor
nodes.summary: At high AoA the descending blade on the right takes a bigger bite than the ascending blade, so thrust is asymmetric and the nose yaws left.

nodes.label: Electrical System
nodes.summary: Belt-driven alternator + battery feeding a bus through a master switch. Loss of the alternator puts you on battery time — shed load.

nodes.label: Alternator vs Generator
nodes.summary: Alternators make rated output at low RPM and are lighter; both are belt-driven and field-excited. Lose the field and you lose charging.

nodes.label: Battery & Master Switch
nodes.summary: The battery starts the engine and buffers the bus; the split master energises the alternator field and the battery contactor.

nodes.label: Bus, Breakers & Ammeter
nodes.summary: Circuit breakers/fuses protect each branch; the ammeter (charge/discharge) or loadmeter tells you the alternator is carrying the load.

nodes.label: Alternator Failure Flow
nodes.summary: Ammeter shows discharge / low-volts light: reset once, then shed nonessential load and treat the battery as a countdown to essential-only.

nodes.label: Fuel System
nodes.summary: Tanks, selector, strainer, pump(s), primer. Sump every drain to catch water and confirm grade/colour (100LL = blue).

nodes.label: Tanks, Selector & Vents
nodes.summary: Feed by gravity (high wing) or pump. Manage the selector to keep the tanks balanced and unported in slips; blocked vents starve the engine.

nodes.label: Fuel Pumps
nodes.summary: Engine-driven pump for normal running; an electric boost/aux pump for start, takeoff, landing and as backup if the primary fails.

nodes.label: Grade, Colour & Contamination
nodes.summary: 100LL is blue, Jet-A is clear/straw — never mix. Sump every drain for water, sediment and correct colour before the first flight of the day.

nodes.label: Starvation vs Exhaustion
nodes.summary: Exhaustion = no fuel left (planning failure). Starvation = fuel aboard but not reaching the engine (selector, vent, pump) — both stop the prop.

nodes.label: Pitot-Static System
nodes.summary: Ram-air pitot feeds the airspeed indicator; the static port feeds altimeter, VSI and ASI. Blockages give classic, predictable failures.

nodes.label: Pitot Tube & Drain
nodes.summary: Ram-air pressure for the ASI. Blocked pitot with an open drain reads zero; blocked pitot AND drain makes the ASI behave like an altimeter — reads high in a climb.

nodes.label: Static Port
nodes.summary: Ambient pressure for altimeter, VSI and ASI. A blocked static port freezes the altimeter and reverses the ASI (reads low in a climb).

nodes.label: Alternate Static Source
nodes.summary: Opens the system to cabin pressure if the port ices over — usually reads slightly high on altitude and airspeed. Pitot heat prevents pitot icing.

nodes.label: Vacuum / Gyro System
nodes.summary: Engine-driven vacuum pump spins the attitude and heading gyros. A quiet failure — cross-check against the electric turn coordinator and partial-panel.

nodes.label: Vacuum Pump & Gauge
nodes.summary: A dry vane pump draws air across the gyros; the suction gauge (~4.5–5.5" Hg) is the only warning you have before the gyros spin down.

nodes.label: Which Gyros It Drives
nodes.summary: Typically the attitude and heading indicators run on vacuum; the turn coordinator is electric — so a vacuum loss leaves you the TC, ASI and altimeter.

nodes.label: Failure & Partial Panel
nodes.summary: The gyros die slowly and lie convincingly — the classic killer. Cover them, fly the electric TC + pitot-static, and get to VFR.

nodes.label: Environmental & Deice
nodes.summary: Heater (exhaust shroud → CO risk), pitot heat, and on capable airplanes boots, hot props or TKS for known ice.

nodes.label: Cabin Heat & CO Risk
nodes.summary: Cabin heat is exhaust warmth through a shroud — a cracked shroud leaks carbon monoxide into the cabin. A CO detector and fresh-air vents are the defense.

nodes.label: Anti-Ice vs De-Ice
nodes.summary: Anti-ice prevents ice forming (heated pitot, hot props, TKS weeping wings); de-ice removes accreted ice (pneumatic boots). Only "known-ice" airplanes may enter icing.

nodes.label: Regulations
nodes.summary: The 14 CFR framework — who may fly, in what, when, and how currency and airworthiness are proven.

nodes.label: Certificates & Ratings
nodes.summary: Student → private → commercial → ATP, plus instrument and category/class ratings. Each unlocks privileges and carries limits.

nodes.label: Medical Certificates
nodes.summary: First/second/third class, or BasicMed. The medical you need matches the privileges you exercise.

nodes.label: Currency & Recency
nodes.summary: Flight review every 24 mo; 3 takeoffs/landings in 90 days for passengers (night = full-stop); 6 approaches + holding + tracking in 6 mo for IFR.

nodes.label: Airworthiness
nodes.summary: The airplane must be in a condition for safe flight and have required inspections done. Pilot and owner share the responsibility.

nodes.label: Required Inspections
nodes.summary: Annual (12 mo), 100-hour if for hire, transponder (24 mo), pitot-static (24 mo for IFR), ELT (battery/12 mo), altimeter.

nodes.label: Required Equipment (ATOMATOFLAMES / FLAPS)
nodes.summary: Day-VFR minimum equipment by memory aid; night adds fuses, landing light, anti-collision, position lights, source of power.

nodes.label: MEL & Inoperative Equipment
nodes.summary: With no MEL, an inop item must be required-or-removed/placarded per 91.213 and a pilot/mechanic determination of safety.

nodes.label: Right-of-Way & Ops Rules
nodes.summary: Who yields (91.113), the 250 KIAS-below-10,000 limit, minimum safe altitudes (91.119), and sterile-cockpit discipline.

nodes.label: Preflight Action (91.103)
nodes.summary: All available information: weather, runway lengths, performance, alternates, fuel, NOTAMs, and for IFR an alternate plan. "NWKRAFT."

nodes.label: Weather
nodes.summary: The atmosphere as a system you read, forecast and stay ahead of.

nodes.label: Weather Theory & Energy
nodes.summary: The "why" beneath every report: where the atmosphere's energy comes from and how it moves heat and water around the planet.

nodes.label: Solar Heating & Uneven Warming
nodes.summary: The sun heats the equator more than the poles, and land faster than water. That temperature imbalance is the engine behind every wind, front and storm.

nodes.label: Global Circulation (Three Cells)
nodes.summary: Hadley, Ferrel and Polar cells carry heat poleward; their boundaries seat the subtropical highs, the prevailing westerlies and the jet stream that steer North American weather.

nodes.label: The Water Cycle & Latent Heat
nodes.summary: Evaporation, condensation and precipitation move water — and the latent heat released when vapor condenses is the actual fuel that builds cumulus into thunderstorms.

nodes.label: Heat Transfer
nodes.summary: Radiation warms the ground, conduction warms the air touching it, convection lifts that air in bubbles, and advection carries whole air masses horizontally — the four ways heat gets around.

nodes.label: Atmosphere & Pressure
nodes.summary: The gas the airplane flies in: its standard state, and how pressure and temperature bend performance.

nodes.label: Standard Atmosphere (ISA)
nodes.summary: The reference day: 15 °C and 29.92" Hg at sea level, cooling ~2 °C per 1,000 ft to the tropopause. Every performance chart is corrected from it.

nodes.label: Density Altitude
nodes.summary: Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature — the altitude the airplane "feels." High, hot and humid = thin air = worse climb, longer roll, higher TAS for the same IAS.

nodes.label: Pressure Altitude
nodes.summary: Height above the standard datum plane (set 29.92). The basis for flight levels and performance charts.

nodes.label: Altimetry & Settings
nodes.summary: Set the local altimeter (QNH) and the altimeter reads field elevation. "High to low, hot to cold, look out below" — flying toward lower pressure or colder air puts you lower than indicated.

nodes.label: Temperature, Lapse Rate & Inversions
nodes.summary: The environmental lapse rate decides stability; a temperature inversion (warmer aloft) caps the air — trapping haze, fog and low-level wind shear beneath it.

nodes.label: Moisture & Stability
nodes.summary: Water and vertical motion — the two ingredients that set the sky's whole mood.

nodes.label: Dewpoint & Humidity
nodes.summary: The closer temperature and dewpoint, the nearer to saturation — a tight spread warns of fog, low ceilings and visible moisture. Cooling to the dewpoint makes cloud.

nodes.label: Stability & Adiabatic Lapse
nodes.summary: Compare the parcel's adiabatic cooling (dry ~3 °C, moist ~1.5 °C /1000 ft) to the environment: parcel warmer than around it → it keeps rising → unstable.

nodes.label: Stable vs Unstable Air
nodes.summary: Stable: stratus, steady precip, poor visibility, smooth ride. Unstable: cumulus, showers, gusts, good visibility, turbulence. Everything downstream flows from this one call.

nodes.label: Clouds
nodes.summary: Grouped by height and by form. "Cirro/alto/strato" name the level, "cumulus" means rising, "stratus" layered, "nimbus" raining — read the sky and you read the stability.

nodes.label: Low Clouds
nodes.summary: Bases below ~6,500 ft: stratus, stratocumulus and rain-bearing nimbostratus. Layered low cloud means stable, moist air — ceilings, poor visibility, a smooth ride.

nodes.label: Middle Clouds
nodes.summary: Roughly 6,500–20,000 ft: altostratus (a grey sheet, sun a dim disc) and altocumulus. Altocumulus castellanus warns of instability aloft and afternoon storms.

nodes.label: High Clouds
nodes.summary: Above ~20,000 ft and made of ice: cirrus, cirrostratus (halo) and cirrocumulus. A thickening, lowering cirrus deck is the first sign of an approaching warm front.

nodes.label: Cumuliform & Vertical
nodes.summary: Cumulus → towering cumulus → cumulonimbus: the growing signature of unstable, convective air. A CB is a thunderstorm with all its hazards, whatever ATIS calls it.

nodes.label: Special Clouds
nodes.summary: Lenticular (standing mountain-wave lens — smooth lift, brutal rotor nearby), rotor, mammatus (hanging pouches under a severe anvil) and fog as stratus at the surface.

nodes.label: Precipitation
nodes.summary: What falls out of the cloud, and what its form tells you about the air it fell through.

nodes.label: Formation
nodes.summary: Drops grow by collision-coalescence in warm cloud, or by the ice-crystal process in cold cloud. It takes cloud, saturation and a way for droplets to grow large enough to fall.

nodes.label: Freezing Rain & Ice Pellets
nodes.summary: A warm layer aloft over a subfreezing layer: rain that freezes on contact (freezing rain) or refreezes into ice pellets first. The classic warm-front icing trap — and a reason to climb.

nodes.label: Snow, Graupel & Hail
nodes.summary: Snow from cold stable air; graupel and hail from strong convective updrafts. Hail can be thrown miles from the parent cell under the anvil.

nodes.label: Virga & Downbursts
nodes.summary: Precipitation that evaporates before reaching the ground — a marker of dry air below and a warning sign for evaporative-cooling downdrafts and dry microbursts.

nodes.label: Air Masses & Fronts
nodes.summary: Big bodies of air with uniform character, and the boundaries where they collide — the engine of most flyable and unflyable weather.

nodes.label: Air Masses
nodes.summary: Named by source region: continental/maritime (dry/moist) × polar/tropical (cold/warm). cP is cold and dry, mT warm and moist — the air mass sets the baseline before any front arrives.

nodes.label: Cold Front
nodes.summary: Cold air undercuts warm, steep and fast: a narrow band of towering cumulus, showers or embedded storms, gusty wind shift, then rapid clearing and cooler, drier air behind.

nodes.label: Warm Front
nodes.summary: Warm air overrides cold, shallow and slow: a wide deck of lowering stratus, steady precipitation, poor visibility and — if the warm air is moist over freezing cold air — freezing rain.

nodes.label: Stationary & Occluded Fronts
nodes.summary: A stationary front stalls with a mix of both regimes; an occlusion forms as a cold front overtakes a warm one, wrapping the worst of both around the low.

nodes.label: Frontal Passage Clues
nodes.summary: Wind shift (veering), temperature and dewpoint change, pressure trend (falling then rising), and the sky sequence — how you know a front went through even without a chart.

nodes.label: Wind & Circulation
nodes.summary: Why the air moves — from the planetary scale down to the ridgeline outside the window.

nodes.label: Pressure Systems
nodes.summary: Highs (sinking, diverging, fair) and lows (rising, converging, cloudy); ridges and troughs are their elongated cousins. Circulation is clockwise-out of a high, counter-clockwise-into a low (N. hemisphere).

nodes.label: Coriolis & Pressure Gradient
nodes.summary: Air accelerates from high to low pressure, then Coriolis deflects it right until the flow parallels the isobars (geostrophic). Tight isobars = strong wind.

nodes.label: Local Winds
nodes.summary: Surface-level effects that override the pressure-gradient wind — the ones that actually decide the ride and the crosswind at your field.

nodes.label: Sea & Land Breeze
nodes.summary: By day the land heats and draws cool air in off the water (sea breeze); by night it reverses. The sea-breeze front can trigger a line of afternoon cumulus along the coast.

nodes.label: Valley & Mountain Winds
nodes.summary: Anabatic (up-slope) winds by day as the sun heats the slopes; katabatic (down-slope) drainage by night as cold dense air slides down — strongest and gustiest near the terrain.

nodes.label: Föhn / Chinook Winds
nodes.summary: Air forced over a ridge dries and warms as it descends the lee side — strong, warm, gusty downslope wind that can raise temperatures and hide turbulence.

nodes.label: Gust Fronts & Outflow
nodes.summary: The cold downdraft of a storm spreads out at the surface as a gust front — a sudden wind shift and shear well ahead of the rain. The shelf cloud is your warning.

nodes.label: Mountain Wave & Rotor
nodes.summary: Stable air over a ridge sets up standing waves with smooth lift aloft but violent rotor and downdrafts below — lenticular and rotor clouds are the visible warning.

nodes.label: Winds Aloft & the Jet Stream
nodes.summary: Wind generally veers and strengthens with height; the jet stream is a fast core near the tropopause bordered by clear-air turbulence.

nodes.label: Hazards
nodes.summary: The weather that actually bends or breaks airplanes.

nodes.label: Thunderstorms
nodes.summary: The complete package: severe turbulence, hail, lightning, downdrafts, low-level wind shear and microbursts. Never fly under one, and give them 20 NM.

nodes.label: Life Cycle
nodes.summary: Cumulus (updraft only) → mature (up + downdrafts, heaviest hazards, the moment it rains) → dissipating (downdraft chokes it off). Needs moisture, instability and a lift trigger.

nodes.label: Types
nodes.summary: Air-mass (single-cell, brief), multicell and squall-line (a wall ahead of a cold front — the worst to cross), and the rotating supercell that spawns tornadoes and giant hail.

nodes.label: Embedded & Night Storms
nodes.summary: Storms hidden in stratiform cloud or darkness — invisible to the eye, so radar, datalink and a wide berth are the only defense.

nodes.label: Turbulence
nodes.summary: Chaotic vertical and horizontal gusts. Below maneuvering speed the wing stalls before it overstresses — slow down and ride it.

nodes.label: Types
nodes.summary: Mechanical (wind over terrain/buildings), thermal/convective (rising bubbles), frontal and wind-shear turbulence, mountain-wave rotor, and clear-air turbulence near the jet.

nodes.label: Wake Turbulence
nodes.summary: The wingtip vortices of a bigger airplane — strongest heavy, clean and slow. Stay above its path and land beyond its touchdown point.

nodes.label: Intensity & Reporting
nodes.summary: Light, moderate, severe, extreme — reported by effect on the airplane and occupants. A PIREP of the ride is worth more than any forecast.

nodes.label: Airframe Icing
nodes.summary: Structural ice needs visible moisture and a surface at 0 °C or below. It destroys lift, adds weight and drag, and jams controls — exit the conditions early.

nodes.label: Rime, Clear & Mixed
nodes.summary: Rime is rough and milky (small drops, fast freeze); clear is smooth, heavy and hard to shed (large drops, slow freeze); mixed is both. Clear and freezing rain are the dangerous ones.

nodes.label: Effects on the Wing
nodes.summary: Even light ice disrupts the airfoil: higher stall speed at a LOWER angle of attack, more drag, less thrust — the stall can come with no warning.

nodes.label: Freezing Rain & Drizzle
nodes.summary: Supercooled drops that freeze on contact — the fastest, heaviest accretion there is. A warm layer aloft over a cold one; climb or descend out, do not press on.

nodes.label: Wind Shear & Microburst
nodes.summary: A sudden change of wind vector with position — brutal on approach and departure, where you have least energy and altitude.

nodes.label: Low-Level Wind Shear
nodes.summary: Near the surface from fronts, inversions, terrain or storms. A shift from headwind to tailwind sinks the airplane and drops the airspeed at the worst moment.

nodes.label: Microburst
nodes.summary: A concentrated column of sinking air under a storm: a strong headwind and updraft, then a violent downdraft and tailwind within a mile or two. Go around early; do not try to fly through it.

nodes.label: Inversion & Frontal Shear
nodes.summary: Wind shear also lurks at temperature inversions and along frontal boundaries away from any storm — smoother-looking air that still bites on final.

nodes.label: Icing Conditions
nodes.summary: Visible moisture (cloud, rain, wet snow) with temperatures near or below freezing — the trigger for both carburetor and airframe ice.

nodes.label: Fog & Restricted Visibility
nodes.summary: Cloud at the surface, plus haze, smoke, blowing snow and dust — the quiet way an easy flight turns into an instrument approach.

nodes.label: Radiation Fog
nodes.summary: Clear, calm, moist nights: the ground radiates heat, cools the air to its dewpoint, and by dawn the valley is socked in. Burns off with the sun and a little wind.

nodes.label: Advection & Upslope Fog
nodes.summary: Advection: moist air moving over a cooler surface (coasts). Upslope: moist air pushed up rising terrain and cooled — both need wind, so they persist longer than radiation fog.

nodes.label: Steam & Precipitation Fog
nodes.summary: Steam fog rises off warm water into cold air; precipitation fog forms as rain saturates cooler air beneath a warm front.

nodes.label: Flight Categories & Minimums
nodes.summary: How reported ceiling and visibility collapse into a single go/no-go word — and the personal line you draw above the legal one.

nodes.label: VFR / MVFR / IFR / LIFR
nodes.summary: Categories by ceiling and visibility: VFR (>3,000 ft & >5 SM), Marginal VFR (1,000–3,000 & 3–5), IFR (500–1,000 & 1–3), Low IFR (<500 ft or <1 SM). The colours on every briefing map.

nodes.label: Ceiling & Sky Cover
nodes.summary: FEW/SCT are not ceilings; the ceiling is the lowest broken or overcast layer (or vertical visibility into an obscuration). It sets MVFR/IFR and whether an approach is legal to start.

nodes.label: Visibility & RVR
nodes.summary: Prevailing visibility in statute miles, or runway visual range in feet where transmissometers exist — the controlling number for approach minimums and takeoff.

nodes.label: Personal Minimums
nodes.summary: Numbers you set on the ground — ceiling, visibility, wind, crosswind — above the regulatory minimums, sized to your currency, the airplane and the day. The heart of good ADM.

nodes.label: Weather Products & Briefing
nodes.summary: How you turn the atmosphere into a go/no-go — the reports you decode and the forecasts you weigh.

nodes.label: Weather Briefing
nodes.summary: Standard, abbreviated or outlook briefings via 1-800-WX-BRIEF / ForeFlight / aviationweather.gov — plus adverse conditions, synopsis, NOTAMs and TFRs. Required preflight information.

nodes.label: METAR
nodes.summary: The hourly (or special, SPECI) surface observation — the ground truth right now, decoded field by field.

nodes.label: Station, Time & Modifier
nodes.summary: ICAO id, a day-hour-minute Zulu timestamp, and AUTO (unattended) or COR (corrected). Everything after is in a fixed order you can read at a glance.

nodes.label: Wind Group
nodes.summary: Direction (true) and speed, gusts after a G, VRB for variable, and a variable range (e.g. 180V240). "00000KT" is calm — watch it against the runway you're using.

nodes.label: Visibility & RVR
nodes.summary: Prevailing visibility in statute miles; runway visual range in feet (R06/2400FT) when it's low. The number that often decides VFR vs IFR.

nodes.label: Present Weather Codes
nodes.summary: Intensity (-/+), descriptor (SH, TS, FZ, BL) and phenomenon (RA, SN, FG, BR, DZ). "-SHRA" is light rain showers; "+TSRA" is a heavy thunderstorm with rain.

nodes.label: Sky Condition & Ceiling
nodes.summary: FEW/SCT/BKN/OVC by hundreds of feet AGL. The ceiling is the lowest BKN or OVC layer — the number the flight category and approach hinge on.

nodes.label: Temp/Dewpoint, Altimeter & Remarks
nodes.summary: Temperature/dewpoint in °C (M for minus), altimeter after an A, then RMK — sea-level pressure, precise readings, and notes the coded body can't hold.

nodes.label: TAF
nodes.summary: The terminal aerodrome forecast — roughly a 5 SM bubble around one airport, and a forecast, not a promise.

nodes.label: Validity & Issue
nodes.summary: Issued four times a day, valid 24 or 30 hours, with a day-hour/day-hour valid period. Amended (TAF AMD) when the forecast busts.

nodes.label: Change Groups
nodes.summary: FM = a rapid, permanent change at a stated time; BECMG = a gradual change over a window; TEMPO = temporary fluctuations under an hour that come and go.

nodes.label: Probability (PROB)
nodes.summary: PROB30/PROB40 flags a 30–40% chance of the stated conditions — usually thunderstorms or low visibility. Not used in the first hours of the forecast.

nodes.label: Reading TAF vs METAR
nodes.summary: The METAR is the airport now; the TAF is that airport's future. Trend the two together, and weight a fresh METAR that already disagrees with the TAF.

nodes.label: Winds & Temps Aloft (FB)
nodes.summary: Forecast wind and temperature at fixed levels — pick the altitude for the best groundspeed, the smoothest ride and the freezing level.

nodes.label: Graphical Forecasts (GFA)
nodes.summary: The Graphical Forecast for Aviation and prog charts — the big-picture map, valid from now out to 15+ hours, that puts the coded reports in spatial context.

nodes.label: PIREPs
nodes.summary: Pilot reports are the only in-situ truth — cloud tops, ride, icing, the actual bases. UA (routine) or UUA (urgent). Give them as freely as you use them.

nodes.label: Radar & Datalink Weather
nodes.summary: The moving picture of the weather — and the traps in how it's made and delivered.

nodes.label: NEXRAD & Echo Intensity
nodes.summary: Ground radar reflectivity in dBZ (green→red→magenta). Composite vs base reflectivity, and attenuation/shadowing behind a strong cell — never pick your way through a line on radar alone.

nodes.label: Satellite Imagery
nodes.summary: Visible shows cloud by daylight; infrared shows cloud-top temperature (so height) day and night — cold, high tops mark the strongest convection.

nodes.label: Datalink & Latency
nodes.summary: FIS-B (ADS-B In) and SiriusXM bring NEXRAD and text products to the cockpit — but the mosaic can be 5–15+ minutes old. Use it strategically, never tactically around a cell.

nodes.label: Lightning & Ceiling/Vis Products
nodes.summary: Lightning networks pinpoint active convection in real time; graphical ceiling and visibility products paint where the IFR is.

nodes.label: In-Flight Advisories
nodes.summary: The warnings issued once you're airborne — matched to the hazard's severity.

nodes.label: AIRMET (S/T/Z)
nodes.summary: Widespread MODERATE hazards for lighter aircraft: Sierra (IFR/mountain obscuration), Tango (turbulence & surface winds) and Zulu (icing & freezing level).

nodes.label: SIGMET
nodes.summary: SEVERE, non-convective hazards for all aircraft — severe turbulence or icing, widespread dust/sand, volcanic ash — issued as needed, not on a schedule.

nodes.label: Convective SIGMET
nodes.summary: Thunderstorm-specific and always implying severe turbulence, icing and low-level wind shear: lines, embedded storms, or areas of heavy convection.

nodes.label: G-AIRMET & CWA
nodes.summary: The graphical, time-stepped G-AIRMET, and the Center Weather Advisory — a short-fuse heads-up for conditions developing over an ARTCC.
